rationale: Ground truth is thin (LGTM approvals plus an informal QA comment confirming boundary/unit/e2e checks), so neither candidate has much substantive human feedback to recall against. X correctly validates the range-consistency logic and flags a minor, low-value gap (no positive unit assertion for layers=2). Y does the same core validation but goes further, tracing the constant through both entrypoints and the pipeline implementation, and surfaces two concrete stale reliability-test files with old hardcoded bounds ('3'/'layers=2 invalid') that would now fail — a specific, plausible, high-value catch mirroring exactly the kind of regression this PR's narrow scope (unit tests only) would miss. Y's findings are more actionable (exact files/lines/diffs) and higher-impact if accurate, giving it better recall of the underlying verification concerns and stronger overall value despite similar precision risk.
